title: Interview Templates
description: Drill the most common algorithmic templates that appear in coding interviews. Each pattern provides a reusable skeleton you can adapt to dozens of problems, so recognizing and applying them quickly becomes second nature.

sections.title: Sliding Window
sections.explanation: The sliding window technique maintains a fixed- or variable-size window over a sequence, sliding it forward one element at a time. Instead of recalculating from scratch, you update the result by subtracting the element leaving the window and adding the one entering. This brings many brute-force O(n*k) problems down to O(n), which interviewers expect you to recognize immediately.
sections.example:
def max_sum_subarray(nums, k):
    window = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window += nums[i] - nums[i - k]
        best = max(best, window)
    return best

print(max_sum_subarray([2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2], 3))  # 9

exercise.prompt: Complete the sliding window function to find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k. Print the result for nums = [2, 3, 1, 5, 4, 2] with k = 3.
exercise.starterCode:
def max_sum_subarray(nums, k):
    window = sum(nums[:k])
    best = window
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window += nums[i] - nums[i - ___]
        best = ___(best, window)
    return best

print(max_sum_subarray([2, 3, 1, 5, 4, 2], 3))
exercise.hint: Subtract the element leaving the window with nums[i - k] and use max(best, window) to track the largest sum seen so far.

sections.title: Binary Search
sections.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration by comparing the middle element to the target. The template uses two pointers, left and right, with a while left <= right loop. Interviewers test edge cases like off-by-one errors and the choice of mid calculation, so drilling this template until it is automatic is essential.

exercise.prompt: Complete the binary search function to find the index of target in a sorted array. Return -1 if not found. Print the result for nums = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12] and target = 10.
exercise.hint: The loop runs while left <= right. When the middle value is too small, move left to mid + 1; when too large, move right to mid - 1.

sections.title: Two Pointers
sections.explanation: The two-pointer technique places one pointer at the start and one at the end of a sorted array, then moves them inward based on a condition. For a pair-sum problem, if the current sum is too small you advance the left pointer; if too large you retreat the right pointer. This eliminates the need for a hash set and solves the problem in O(n) time with O(1) space, a tradeoff interviewers love to discuss.

exercise.prompt: Complete the two-pointer function to find a pair of indices whose values sum to target in a sorted array. Print the result for nums = [1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 11] and target = 10.
exercise.hint: When the sum is too small, increment left by 1 to increase it. When too large, decrement right by 1 to decrease it.

sections.title: Prefix Sum
sections.explanation: A prefix sum array stores the cumulative sum up to each index, turning any range-sum query into a constant-time subtraction. Building the prefix array is O(n), and each query is O(1), which is a dramatic improvement over recalculating from scratch. Interviewers use this pattern in subarray-sum problems, and it often serves as a building block inside more complex solutions.

exercise.prompt: Complete the prefix sum builder and use it to answer a range sum query. Print the sum of elements from index 1 to index 3 (inclusive) for nums = [2, 5, 1, 7, 3].
exercise.hint: Each prefix entry builds on the previous one: prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + nums[i]. The range sum from l to r is prefix[r + 1] - prefix[l].

sections.title: Backtracking
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores every candidate solution by choosing an option, recursing deeper, then undoing the choice to try the next option. This choose-explore-unchoose pattern is the backbone of permutation, combination, and constraint-satisfaction problems in interviews. Pruning branches early keeps the search tractable, and interviewers expect you to articulate the time complexity in terms of the decision tree.

exercise.prompt: Complete the backtracking function to generate all permutations of [1, 2, 3]. Print the resulting list of permutations.
exercise.hint: Skip the chosen element by slicing remaining[:i] + remaining[i+1:]. After the recursive call, undo the choice with path.pop().
